title: Melicope villosa
intro: Melicope villosa, also known as Hawaiian neem or Māmaki, is a shrub native to the Hawaiian Islands, belonging to the family Rutaceae. The plant has been traditionally used in Hawaiian medicine for its various health benefits, including anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and antimicrobial properties, which can help alleviate symptoms of arthritis, skin conditions, and respiratory issues. The bioactive constituents of Melicope villosa include flavonoids, alkaloids, and limonoids, such as quercetin and marmesin, which are responsible for the plant's therapeutic effects. In traditional Hawaiian medicine, Melicope villosa is often prepared as a tea, tincture, or infused oil, and is used to treat a range of health conditions, including fever, coughs, and skin irritations.
taxonomy: Melicope villosa is classified within the family Rutaceae, commonly known as the citrus family. This plant is native to Hawaii and is also known as the hairy melicope. It belongs to the genus Melicope, which consists of several species of flowering plants. Melicope villosa is further divided into several subspecies, each with distinct characteristics. The classification of Melicope villosa is based on its morphology, anatomy, and molecular characteristics.
